coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of biomass derived economically viable carbon dot polymer nanocomposite: A perspective towards sustainable removal of dyes from synthetic wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Momina, Momina
Ahmad, Kafeel
Khan, Zahid A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a sustainable carbon dot-based polymer nanocomposite was synthesized for the removal of organic contaminants, such as dyes, with the aim of addressing environmental concerns. Throughout the formulation process of polymer nanocomposites, the agglomeration of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix poses a difficult challenge. Therefore, iron oxide particles after coating with carbon dots particles were seamlessly integrated into the polymer matrix. The chemical, physical, optical, and thermal stabilities of the formulated composites were studied (FTIR, XRD, FESEM/TEM, EDX, UV–vis, AFM, and fluorescence analyses), which showed the uniform dispersion of nanoparticles on the polymer matrix. This approach of synthesizing composites using naturally derived materials will contribute to the decomposition of used adsorbents, promoting environmentally conscious and sustainable product practices. Furthermore, this research also focuses on significant adsorption parameters and their optimization using the Taguchi design approach for wastewater remediation. Validation of the adsorption process by isotherm study showed that the maximum adsorption capacity of PANI-FOCD was 462.96 mg/g. In addition, the performance of the composite was analyzed in terms of its economic feasibility and continuous adsorption efficiency, which ensured its sustainability in practical applications.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of biomass derived economically viable carbon dot polymer nanocomposite: A perspective towards sustainable removal of dyes from synthetic wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi-110025, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi-110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a sustainable carbon dot-based polymer nanocomposite was synthesized for the removal of organic contaminants, such as dyes, with the aim of addressing environmental concerns. Throughout the formulation process of polymer nanocomposites, the agglomeration of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix poses a difficult challenge. Therefore, iron oxide particles after coating with carbon dots particles were seamlessly integrated into the polymer matrix. The chemical, physical, optical, and thermal stabilities of the formulated composites were studied (FTIR, XRD, FESEM/TEM, EDX, UV–vis, AFM, and fluorescence analyses), which showed the uniform dispersion of nanoparticles on the polymer matrix. This approach of synthesizing composites using naturally derived materials will contribute to the decomposition of used adsorbents, promoting environmentally conscious and sustainable product practices. Furthermore, this research also focuses on significant adsorption parameters and their optimization using the Taguchi design approach for wastewater remediation. Validation of the adsorption process by isotherm study showed that the maximum adsorption capacity of PANI-FOCD was 462.96 mg/g. In addition, the performance of the composite was analyzed in terms of its economic feasibility and continuous adsorption efficiency, which ensured its sustainability in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are prevalent pollutants in the textile industry, with over 15 % of dyes used in the dyeing process ending up in the environment. This discharge raises serious environmental concerns, including a decline in water quality due to increased oxygen demand in the form of TOC, BOD, and COD; biodiversity loss due to reduced water photosynthesis; and risks to human health, especially from the mutagenic and carcinogenic effects of dyes such as methyl orange (MO). Although various methods exist for dye removal, adsorption stands out because of its cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and efficiency [1–3].

Various types of adsorbents have been synthesized to improve their adsorption performance and regeneration efficiency for wastewater treatment. However, their large-scale adoption across industries still needs to mark their footprint due to lower economic viability, which generally depends on higher adsorption capacity and regeneration performance [4]. Therefore, innovative approaches for synthesizing adsorbents are required to support efficient removal of water contaminants. Conducting polymers, such as polyaniline (PANI), have been widely investigated owing to their simple synthesis, good adsorption capabilities, nontoxicity, doping de-doping reversibility, low cost, and environmental stability. However, the main challenge in conducting polymeric adsorbents is their mechanical strength and poor regeneration, which can be improved by impregnating them with magnetic nanoparticles. Researchers have shown great interest in magnetic separation technologies for water and wastewater treatment applications. These materials exhibit excellent adsorption capabilities and can be easily separated using an external magnetic field. Nevertheless, its adsorption performance still requires some improvement because it exhibits agglomeration behavior, ultimately affecting its adsorption capacity [5,6]. Intensive collaborative research has focused on incorporating or impregnating magnetic nanoparticles in a polymer matrix [7] to improve the physical, chemical, mechanical, and functional characteristics of composites for wastewater treatment. The agglomeration behavior of magnetic nanoparticles in a polymer matrix decreases the interface volume and performance [8]. It can be improved by grafting or coating nanofillers with functional groups, such as OH and COOH, to boost the quality dispersion and attachment to the polymer matrix [9].

The addition of Carbon dots (CDs) in this study is of significant importance. CDs have gained substantial attention in recent research due to their unique properties, simple synthesis, and remarkable efficiency in dealing with dye pollutants [10]. These zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit outstanding photoluminescence characteristics, possess abundant functional groups, offer environmental sustainability, provide a large surface area, are cost-effective, and can be easily synthesized [11]. Their compatibility with various matrices allows the creation of CD-based nanocomposites through covalent bonding or intermolecular interactions [12]. In the context of wastewater treatment, which often requires ideal solid matrices such as polymers and magnetic nanoparticles for the efficient separation of adsorbents via external magnetic fields [13,14], the integration of CDs is particularly promising. Previous studies have successfully used magnetic nanoparticles coated with CDs to remove specific contaminants such as benzo[a] pyrene, carbamazepine, and tetracycline from water [15–17]. Few studies have been conducted on combining polymer matrices with CDs for wastewater treatment. For example, nGO-CDs combined with chitosan (CS) and cellulose showed 99 % and 100 % removal of diclofenac sodium [18], and NGO-CDs coated with a cellulose filter have been used for methyl blue with an adsorption capacity of 3.6 mg/g [12] etc. Here, a ternary composite was synthesized using CDs, iron oxide nanoparticles, and PANI, demonstrating the potential of CDs as key components in efficient dye adsorption processes. This enhances the adsorption capacity and contributes to the material's economic viability for wastewater treatment.

Moreover, a good adsorption capacity and reusability of the material can be achieved by optimizing the adsorption parameters using a simple optimization technique. Experimental techniques such as the Taguchi design can be employed in this regard, as they provide optimum results using the minimum number of experiments [19]. Taguchi's design approach is a simple systematic approach for optimizing adsorbent performance in water treatment applications. It is based on statistical approaches that reduce the time required for experimental investigations by considering multiple factors to analyze which factor is most significant for the output response [20].

Based on the above discussion, this work introduces a novel approach involving the fabrication of a carbon-dot-based polymer nanocomposite through an in situ polymerization method. In this process, Fe3O4 (FO) and CD are deposited onto PANI, forming PANI-FOCD, effectively mitigating the agglomeration challenges commonly associated with nanoparticles within a polymer matrix. This is a critical advancement because nanoparticle agglomeration significantly reduces the efficiency of the adsorbent and, consequently, its overall economic feasibility. Notably, this study utilizes an eco-friendly synthesis method for carbon dots directly from biomass, specifically apple peel, eliminating the need for toxic or complicated post-treatment processes. In addition, extensive characterization of the resulting adsorbent has been carried out to elucidate its chemical, physical, thermal, and optical properties, its utilization in dye removal from wastewater, continuous adsorption studies, and the implementation of the Taguchi design approach to optimize the adsorption parameters for superior dye removal. The synthesized composite exhibited enhanced economic viability, as demonstrated through a comprehensive cost analysis in Section 3.9.7.

Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), aniline monomer (C6H5NH2, 99.5 %), anionic dye methyl orange MO (85 %, C14H14N3NaO3S), brilliant green BG (∼90 %, C27H34N2O4S), rhodamine B (RhB, 97 %, C28H31ClN2O3), methyl violet MV (∼90 %, C24H28N3Cl), and methyl red MR (≥85 %, C15H15N3O2) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium persulfate (KPS, 99.9 %, K2S2O8), sodium hydroxide (97 %, NaOH), hydrochloric acid, (35–37 %, HCl), and potassium nitrate (KNO3) were purchased from Merck, India. Ethanol ( > 99 %, C2H5OH) was purchased from Fine chemicals. Dilution of the dye solution and washing of the adsorbent were performed using double-distilled water (DDW).

CDs were prepared via a facile green solvo-thermal method using apple peel as the carbon source. Apple peels were chopped into small pieces and dried in an oven at 80 °C. Dried peels were ground, mixed at a 75:25 v/v ratio of DDW:C2H5OH, and transferred to an autoclave reactor heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The dark brown solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min and filtration through a 0.45 μm filter to remove large solid particles after cooling the solution from the autoclave reactor.

FO nanoparticles were prepared using a co-precipitation method, and the synthesis procedure has been reported in our recent publication [7]. FeCl3·6H2O and FeSO4·7H2O (0.1 M) were mixed together at a specific ratio of 1:2 for 30 min. 1 M NaOH solution was added dropwise to the iron solution and stirred for 2–3 h. This results in the formation of a black precipitate. The resulting solution was further washed and centrifuged until the pH reached 7. The final product was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h.

The prepared FO nanoparticles (0.1 g) were mixed with 10 mL of the prepared CDs solution and sonicated for 1 h to completely coat the dots onto the nanoparticles. After sonication, the product was separated using an external magnet, and the iron oxide nanoparticles coated with carbon dots were rinsed with deionized distilled water (DDW) and then dried in an oven at 70 °C.

The aniline monomer (0.4 M) was mixed with 75 mL of a 1 M HCl solution and sonicated for 30 min. 3.3 g of KPS solution and solution of FOCD solution were combined with 25 mL of 1 M HCl solution and subjected to sonication in a bath for 30 min. Both the solutions were mixed under continuous stirring for 7 h in an ice bath (0–5 °C). The resulting green solution is subsequently subjected to washing and centrifugation at a speed of 7000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for a duration of 10 min. The obtained product was kept in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The green product was converted to a smooth powder using a mortar and pestle. A schematic of the synthesis of the ternary composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

The functional groups of the ternary composites were analyzed before and after adsorption by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) using PerkinElmer Spectrometer version 10.4. The crystallinity and amorphous nature of the composite were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku TTRAX III diffractometer. The textural and morphological properties of the composite, along with elemental distribution analysis, were examined by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) using JEOL-JEM 2100, Zeiss Gemini 300 and JEOL-JSM 6510 instruments. Elemental analysis and the oxidation state of the composite were determined by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III. The magnetic properties of the composite were investigated using a Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) equipped with Lake Shore 7410 Series VSM equipment. The optical properties of the composites were examined by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Hitachi U3900) and PL spectroscopy (Horiba DeltaFlex01-DD). Surface area of composite was calculated using N2 adsorption desorption method known as Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (Autosorb I) with degassing temperature of 100 °C for 3 h and pore size distribution is determined on the basis of Barrett, Joyner, and Halenda (BJH) and density functional theory (DST) method. Thermal analysis of the composites was conducted by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in a nitrogen atmosphere using a thermogravimetric analyzer (Perkinelmer pyris) at a rate of 20 °C/min.

The adsorption parameters were investigated through a series of batch experiments, exploring factors such as the quantity of adsorbent used, concentration of the adsorbate, pH of the adsorbate solution, duration of the adsorption process, and temperature of the adsorbate solution. At a known concentration of 50 mL dye solution 10–20 mg of adsorbent was suspended and stirred at a constant speed of 100 rpm at various pH values. After adsorption, each solution is filtered, and final concentration of MO dye (equilibrium concentration) has been evaluated by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at λmax = 464 nm. The maximum wavelengths of BG, RhB, MV, and MR were 624, 550, 590, and 525 nm, respectively. The adsorption capacity and adsorption efficiency were measured using Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively [7].

A continuous adsorption study is performed in downflow mode using a borosilicate glass column with a diameter of 10 mm and a length of 150 mm. The adsorbent was filled in a glass column, and synthetic dye solution was passed through it at a flow rate of 0.2–0.8 mL/min using a peristaltic pump (Ravel Hiteks, Pvt. Ltd.; Model: RH-P100VS-100). The dye solution was collected at regular intervals and was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. All the experiments were performed twice, and their results are mentioned as mean standard deviation. The schematic arrangement of the continuous adsorption study is shown in Fig. S1.

It's important to note that the elimination of colored compounds from synthetic wastewater through adsorption relies on several factors, including pH, the amount of adsorbent used, the initial concentration of the dye, the duration of contact, and the temperature. It is crucial to analyze the parametric effect on the removal of dye and their optimization to obtain the optimum adsorption performance of PANI-FOCD. For this purpose, the Taguchi design, developed by Dr. Taguchi, has been used to optimize the input parameters and analyze the overall effect of parameters on the process [21]. It is considered a reliable and efficient methodology that optimizes the process conditions using statistical techniques. Taguchi method uses an orthogonal array (OA) to explore the effect of different combinations of input parameters on the output response using a minimum number of experiments, which is necessary to optimize a process or system [22,23]. The number of columns in OA represents the number of input and their interaction, whereas the number of rows indicates the number of experiments to be performed. The total count of runs or experiments (N) is determined by the chosen quantity of parameters (P) and their corresponding levels (L), as indicated in Eq. (3) [24].
(3)
N =
L − 1
P + 1

Taguchi design involves use of the following steps: (1) choosing the input variables and their levels, (2) choosing the output variable or response of the process, (3) selection of an appropriate OA and allocation of input variables and their interactions to OA, (4) execution of experiments as per OA, (5) analyzing the experimental results using signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio, analysis of means (ANOM), and analysis of variance (ANOVA), (6) deciding the optimal setting of the input variables, and (7) application of confirmation test to validate the optimisation outcome.

A loss function can assess the deviation of the experimental output variable value from the desired value in the Taguchi design. This loss function can further be adapted to the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio, where the term “signal” corresponds to the targeted value and “noise” corresponds to the undesired response value [21]. The S/N ratio is a logarithmic function value classified as larger-the-better, nominal-the-better, and smaller-the-better, which Eqn defines. (4), Eq. (5) and Eq. (6), respectively [21].

Three input parameters of the adsorption process, each at three levels, were considered for examining their effects on dye adsorption on PANI-FOCD. Twenty-seven experiments as per L27 (313) OA have been conducted. The input parameters and their levels are given in Table 1 . Input parameters A, B, and C were assigned to columns 2, 5, and 6 of the OA, respectively. The maximum adsorption capacity of the composite was achieved by choosing the S/N ratio for larger-the-better criteria.

ANOVA was employed to establish the significant impact of input parameters and their interactions on the resultant response. The calculations involved the determination of the Total Sum of Squares (SST), Sum of Squares of Input Parameters (SSI), and Sum of Squares of Error (SSerror), along with their corresponding degrees of freedom (dfT, dfI, dferror), computed using the equations outlined in Table S1. Correspondingly, Mean Square Error for each input parameter (MSI), Mean Square Error (MSerror), F-ratio, and the percentage contribution of input parameters are also computed utilizing the equations presented in Table S1. In instances where the F-ratio value surpassed the critical F-ratio value (derived from the F-distribution table with a significance level α = 0.05 and the preferred degrees of freedom df), it is established that the associated input parameters and their interactions significantly influenced the output response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of composite by isotherm and kinetics study
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption isotherm is an expression between the amount of adsorbate adsorbed on composite and equilibrium concentration. This study uses three types of isotherms: Langmuir, Temkin, and Freundlich isotherms. The parameters of the isotherm model have been evaluated by a non-linear optimization method to reduce the error functions. Eq. (9), Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) defines the non-linear equations of the model [7].

where, Qmax is maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g) of PANI-FOCD, KL (L/mg) is Langmuir term, which is related to interaction energies, KF [(mg/g)/(mg/L) n] and n are Freundlich coefficients related to Freundlich affinity coefficient, and B & KT are Temkin constant (=RT/b), and maximum adsorption energy. b [(J/mol). (g/mg)] is heat of adsorption, T is the absolute temperature (K) and R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K).

Three kinetic models specifically; pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intraparticle diffusion model are applied to understand the adsorption mechanism. These models are defined by Eq. (12), Eq. (13) and Eq. (14) [7] as given below:

These constants are calculated by plotting log (qe-qt) vs t, qt vs t, and qt vs t0.5.

FTIR analysis of CD, PANI-FO, and PANI-FOCD are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The broad band of CD observed at 3447 cm−1 has been occurring due to NH or OH stretching vibrations. Moreover, peaks at 2854 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, 1524 cm−1, and 1177 cm−1 occur due to stretching vibration of CH, CC, CN and CO bonds existing on CDs. COC group's symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration bands are observed at 1418 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1. Saturated alkyl groups (υC-H epoxy) and anhydrides (γC-O epoxy) are present at 2864 cm−1 and 904 cm−1 [25]. Also, PANI-FO and PANI-FOCD peak at 3400 cm−1, corresponding to NH stretching vibration due to the protonation of nitrogen atoms in PANI. The broadness of the peak in PANI-FOCD can occur due to the presence of CDs on composite [7]. The characteristic peaks related to the quinoid structure's stretching vibration and the benzenoid ring's deformation in PANI manifest at 1563 cm−1 and 1489 cm−1. The stretching of CNC bonds and stretching vibration of CN bonds occur at 1300 cm−1 and 1238 cm−1 in PANI-FO and PANI-FOCD, respectively. The delocalization of π-electrons through PANI protonation is observed at a characteristic peak of 1158 cm−1 [25]. The peaks in the range 500–600 cm−1 show the presence of FeO groups [26]. Similarly, the characteristic peaks of CDs, such as symmetric and asymmetric groups of COC corresponding to CDs, have been observed at 1418 cm−1. Further, CN and CC bonds, which have been shifted slightly in PANI-FOCD, are also observed. However, the intensity of some peaks is lower than that of pure CDs, which confirms the presence of CDs and their interaction with PANI [27].

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2(b)) for the formulated FO with diffraction angle at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.5°, 57°, and 63.17° corresponding to miller indices (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440). These miller indices plane agrees with JCPDS Card No: 19–0629. The characteristic peaks of FO diminish after the incorporation of FO with CD and PANI. Moreover, diffraction peaks at 21.68°, 28°, 30°, 33°, 37°, 43.12°, 46.27° of PANI-FO are corresponding to miller indices of (020), (200), (220), (311), (202), (302), and (440) [7]. The peaks witnessed for PANI-FOCD are much sharper than PANI-FO and FO, which indicates the formation of a new morphological structure of composite and crystalline structure [15]. The new peaks at 2θ = 26–28.2° can occur due to the presence of graphitic carbon (sp2) of CD corresponding to the diffraction of (002) planes [28,29]. The characteristic peaks of FO at 35.7° appear in PANI-FOCD, which also confirms the presence of the Fe group in the composite.

The results of TGA analysis for the composite with and without CDs are illustrated in Fig. 2(c). The TGA graph of PANI-FO has been divided into three segments: 150-200 °C, 300–550 °C, and 550–800 °C. The first transition of 0.46 mg weight loss in PANI-FO composite is due to the evaporation of water molecules. The second transition shows that 0.33 mg of weight loss can be attributed to an acid dopant associated with the PANI backbone. The third transition occurring at 300–800 °C offers 3.17 mg and 0.15 mg of weight loss, which may correspond to oxidative degradation of PANI in an oxygen atmosphere. This stage might also represent the reduction in interfacial interaction between the polymer and nanoparticle due to the destruction of the polymer chain. Concerning the PANI-FOCD composite, the first transition state corresponds to the evaporation of water molecules associated with CD and PANI. The second and third transition stages in CD coated composite appeared at 200–350 °C and 350–450 °C, respectively, higher than PANI-FO, owing to strong interfacial interaction between the PANI and nanoparticles. The fourth transition stage (450–800 °C) may occur due to the decarboxylation of carboxyl functional groups attached to the surface of CD and results in structural collapse with weight loss of 2.18 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Textural and morphological properties of composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3(a) shows the HRTEM image of CD depicting proper dispersion in solution, spherical shape, and uniform in size with diameter ranging from 8 to 10 nm (Fig. 3(b)). Further, CDs' lattice spacing is around 0.236 nm, similar to graphite carbon's basal spacing [30,31]. After coating CDs on FO nanoparticles, the TEM images show that the particle size increases and ranges from 8 to 14 nm (Fig. 3(c)). The lattice spacing of FOCD has been increased, which can be due to the coupling of CD with FO nanoparticles [30]. Fig. 3(d) and 3(e) represent the HRTEM images of composite PANI-FOCD. Images show that FOCD nanoparticles are successfully incorporated into a polymer matrix and have lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, 0.236 nm, and 0.345 nm. Moreover, the composite's particle size distribution shows the composite's average diameter of particles is ∼34 nm (Fig. 3(f)). Surface treatment of FO nanofillers with small-sized CDs helps reduce nanofillers' agglomeration. This leads to an increase in interfacial reaction between the polymer matrix and the nanofiller [32]. This is necessary for an adsorbent's strong adsorption performance.

FESEM and SEM analysis are used to comprehend the surface morphology of materials. When examined, the PANI-FOCD composite's surface morphology shows that the surface has a rough and porous structure where dye molecules can be adsorbed. The integration of CDs may make the porous structure of the composite conceivable. The carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CDs help to successfully attach to PANI through electrostatic attraction and show uniform dispersion onto the polymer matrix [33]. EDX mapping confirms the presence of each component in the composite Fig. 4 (d,e,f,g). The uniform dispersion of carbon (68.37 wt%), oxygen (12.69 wt%), Nitrogen (7.9 wt%), Cl (4.1 wt%), and Fe (7.01 wt%) has been observed from EDS mapping onto polymer matrix. Carbon and oxygen are present in all the components, including PANI, CD, and FO (as shown in Fig. S2). Nitrogen comes from amine groups of PANI and CD. Further, the presence of Fe is occurring due to the dispersion of iron oxide nanoparticles. Cl might be occurring because HCl is used as a solvent for the in-situ polymerization of PANI. Fig. 4 (g) displays the SEM of composite after adsorption, which shows that the pores of the composite have been filled. The elemental mapping of the composite after adsorption (Fig. S2) indicates the presence of S compounds, which may have occurred due to SO3 groups in MO dye.

The confirmation of the PANI-FOCD hybrid nanocomposite formation was also validated by analyzing two- and three-dimensional AFM images. The granular rough morphology with nano pores of the nanocomposite indicates a uniform distribution of nanofillers on the PANI surface, which self-assembled through electrostatic interaction between positively charged sites of PANI and negatively charged sites of FOCD. These findings align well with SEM and TEM results. The estimated average roughness of the PANI-FOCD nanocomposite film is approximately 119 nm (Fig. 5).

The elemental states and surface functional groups of CDs are determined by XPS analysis. The three peaks at 283 eV, 301 eV, and 530 eV in the XPS survey scan correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, which shows that CDs have carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with an atomic percentage of 78.41 %, 6.5 %, and 19.08 % (as shown in Fig. S3 (a)). The nitrogen content is higher than in the reported literature [34], contributing to surface passivation of CDs. The high-resolution spectra of C1s show three peaks (Fig. S3(b)): 281.8 eV corresponding to CC, 283.06 eV attributing to CN, and 286.09 eV assigning to CO bonds [35]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s show two peaks (Fig. S3(c)); 530 eV set to COH/COC, and another small peak at 531 eV could be due to CO [34,36]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s can be resolved into two peaks (Fig. S3(d)): 396.83 eV corresponding to CN, and 398.76 eV attributing to CN [37].

The XPS survey scan of the composite, as shown in Fig. 6(a), shows five peaks which correspond to Cl2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p at 195 eV, 280 eV, 398 eV, 528 eV, and 708 eV of binding energies. Elements of carbon and nitrogen originate from polymer backbone and carbon dots groups, whereas chlorine elements are due to hydrochloric acid, which is used for the synthesis of composite. For C1s (Fig. 6(b)) is deconvoluted into three peaks of CC/CC, which is due to sp2 bonded carbon PANI and CDs, CO/CN are due to carbonyl groups in PANI, and CO is due to carboxyl groups with binding energies of 283 eV, 285 eV, and 289.7 eV, respectively. These carbon peaks are in good agreement with the literature [38]. Characterization of composite PANI-FOCD of deconvoluted N1s (Fig. 6(c)) spectra by XPS analysis shows the presence of N- with a binding energy of 397 eV corresponding to quinonoid imine, –NH- with a binding energy of 399 eV due to availability of benzenoid amine and NH+ is due to the protonated amine with a binding energy of 401.1 eV [39,40]. The high-resolution spectra of Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 (Fig. 6(d)) with binding energies of 708.84 eV and 722.43 eV are linked with iron oxide nanoparticles in the Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation states with a spin energy separation of 14 eV [41]. The deconvolution of O1s (Fig. 6(e)) spectra depicts the two peaks at 529 eV and 530 eV, corresponding to FeO and OH groups, respectively.

VSM analysis is used to corroborate the magnetic behavior of the composite before and after the addition of CDs. Fig. 7 (a) represents the magnetic hysteresis curve, which represents the ferromagnetism property of composite with mean coercive force (Hc) of 34.19 Oe and magnetic remanence (Mr) of 8.94 emu/g. The saturation magnetization of the composite is found to be 28.09 emu/g. The magnetization property of a composite has been reduced as compared to FO because of the incorporation of PANI and CDs, which do not retain any magnetic behavior [7,42].

Carbon dots are biocompatible in nature and possess excellent photoluminescence chemical and physical properties. These CDs are prepared from natural-based materials; in this study, it is apple peel, a precursor of glucose and less toxic. Therefore, these properties of CDs prompted us to coat over FO nanoparticles. The formation of a layer of CDs over FO nanoparticles is confirmed by UV–Vis analysis and photoluminescence analysis (Fig. 7(b–d)). The UV–Vis analysis of CDs shows π-π* transition of CC at 280 nm and n-π* transition of CO at 310 nm. These absorption peaks are similar to the carbon dots in literature [43]. Moreover, the absorption peaks of FO nanoparticles at 280 nm show the formation of the nanoparticle, and similar results are reported in the literature [44]. The absorption peaks of FOCD increase and some new peaks have been observed (320 nm to 370 nm), which corresponds to the formation of CD coating over FO nanoparticles. The energy band gap (Ebg) of nanomaterial and CDs can be calculated by Tauc plot using Eq. (15) [44];
(15)
αhϑ = B
hϑ −
E bg
γ / 2
where Ebg, α , ϑ , and B are energy band gap, absorption coefficient, light frequency, and proportionality constants. The value of γ (=1/2, 2), depends on electronic transition of material. The Ebg values are determined by extrapolating the linear parts of the curve. The Ebg of FO, CDs, and FOCD nanoparticles are calculated to be 5.82 eV, 3.51 eV, and 3.06 eV for direct and indirect electronic transitions, respectively. However, the absorption peaks of PANI/FOCD have shown some changes, except for the absorption peaks of FO and CDs at 280 nm. All other peaks of FO and CDs are suppressed in composite, which indicates that there is some interaction between FOCD and PANI. The disappearance of absorption peaks after the formation of the composite can occur due to physical blending or in-situ polymerization of PANI [45]. These results confirm that strong electronic interactions are present [45] between doped PANI and FOCD. The Ebg values of the resultant nanocomposites are found to be 5.56 eV.

The maximum peak or the PL intensity of CDs is observed at 470 nm when excited at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which is higher than the PL intensity of emission at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were also studied at different excitation wavelengths, which suggests that the emission wavelength increases and shifts (460 nm to 485 nm) as the excitation wavelength increases from 270 nm to 380 nm, as shown in Fig. S4. The availability of photo-induced electrons and holes at different energy levels in CDs can be responsible for the shifting of emission spectra [46]. Emission spectra also confirm the formation of a coating of CDs over FO at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 6(d)). The fluorescence quenching has been observed after adding CDs in FO nanoparticles. It can be due to the coordination of Fe3+ ions with the carbonyl groups of carbon dots, which indicates that the coating of carbon dots covers iron oxide nanoparticles. The carbonyl group's excited state electrons are moved to the vacant d orbitals of the FO and follow a fluorescence quenching mechanism [15]. The emission spectra of PANI-FOCD shows the highest luminescence intensity compared to FOCD and CD (Fig. 7(c)). The broad emission has been observed over a range of 420–490 nm for PANI-FOCD at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Generally, the benzenoid ring is responsible for the photoluminescence of PANI due to π-π* transition at 330 nm, and sometimes, the quinoid groups quench the photoluminescence due to intrachain energy dissipation [47]. However, in this study, the maximum emission of composite is observed at around 460 nm, which can be understood due to the delocalized π-conjugated system. As per the literature, the photoluminescence of PANI is a singlet exciton-dominated process and does not produce a spin-flip mechanism necessary for optical transitions [48]. Therefore, the π-π* transition of the benzenoid ring of PANI is mainly responsible for maximum emission. Although there is no mechanism of triplet excitation in conducting polymers, higher emission can occur due to higher π-conjugations [45]. Also, the crystallinity of PANI-FOCD increased, as shown by XRD results; this leads to the fact that the benzenoid and quinoid ring arrangement in the unit has been improved, which results in higher π-conjugations. All these features and characteristics show a strong interconnection between PANI and FOCD nanoparticles.

The performance of an adsorbent depends on the specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume. The surface area and pore size of the composite were measured by BET analysis. The composite has micro and mesopores, according to the adsorption-desorption isotherm and type-IV isotherm. The interaction between gas molecules and composite leads to capillary condensation. In this case, the desorption path differs from the adsorption path, makes a hysteresis loop, and follows a type IV isotherm, as shown in Fig. S5. At relatively low pressure, the increase in N2 uptake upsurges but reaches a plateau at relatively high pressure of P/Po = 812. The BET surface area of the composite is 111.23 m2/g. This composite exhibits a wider hysteresis loop of the H4 type, which has the characteristics of slit-like pores with various distributions of pore size and diameter [49]. Multiple techniques such as BJH, Dubinin-Radushkevich (DR), Horvath and Kawazoe (HK), and DFT determine the complete pore size distribution of such materials. BJH method is generally applied for mesoporous size analysis based on the Kelvin eq. DR and HK methods are used for micro-pores analysis but cannot be applied to mesoporous.

In contrast to these approaches, the DFT method is the realistic approach for pore size distribution, which is not only used for micro-pore analysis but also provides a more convincing description of the thermodynamic properties of the pore fluid [50]. The non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) approach for pore size distribution is shown in Fig. S5. The NLDFT approach shows a total pore volume of 0.017 cc/g and a pore size of 16.033 Å (1.603 nm). Moreover, the BJH pore size distribution shows that the total volume is 0.021 cc/g. BJH pore size distribution does not provide a true density profile and also reduces the pore size of 9.71 Å (0.97 nm) compared to the NLDFT method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs concentration on removal of dye
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary studies on the adsorption of MO dye have been performed at different concentrations of CDs in the composite. This study shows that the highest dye adsorption is achieved at 10 mL CD, which can be due to the slowest rate of electron-hole combination of CDs, as is evident from UV–Vis analysis (shown in Fig. 8(a)). Since the concentration of CD =10 mL in composite shows higher dye adsorption efficiency, it is used for the rest of the experiments. Moreover, in preliminary studies, dye adsorption concerning temperature (30–60 °C) has been studied, which shows that maximum adsorption occurs at temperature 30 °C, as shown in Fig. 8 (b). It means that dye adsorption is unfavorable at high temperatures and does not oblige supplementary energy. Moreover, the interaction between the PANI-FOCD and dye reduces, resulting in lower adsorption capacity than at lower temperatures. In all cases, the equilibrium is achieved in 90–105 min.

This current investigation aims to optimize the adsorption capacity of the ternary composite PANI-FOCD for the adsorption of MO dye. A larger-the-better model has been chosen for which the S/N ratio has been calculated from Eq. (4). The three-level-three factor orthogonal matrix is given in Table 2 . The experimental value of the adsorption capacity and its corresponding S/N ratio (calculated for each run) are also given in Table 2. The maximum (233.54 mg/g) and the minimum (9.46 mg/g) adsorption capacity are obtained for the 25th and 12th runs, respectively. The optimum parameters are determined by ANOM results, as depicted in Table 3 .

The mean S/N (ηm) ratio has been calculated by taking the mean of the S/N ratio of factors at a particular level. For example, ηm at level 1 of factor A is 34.64, which is calculated by taking the mean of the S/N ratio of run no. 1,2,3,10,11,12,19,20 and 21. Similarly, ηm at level 2 of factor A is 37.44, determined by taking the mean S/N ratio of run no. 4,5,6,13,14,15,22,23, and 24. All the ηm values of factors A, B, and C at levels 1, 2, and 3 have been evaluated similarly. The input parameters are assessed using these ηm values, determining their ranking by computing the range between the highest and lowest ηm value associated with each input parameter. The input parameter that has the biggest difference in values is given the top rank (rank 1). As the difference in values became smaller, the rank also went down.

Therefore, in this study, the maximum difference value is 20.97 for the input parameter C, i.e., initial dye concentration, whereas A (pH) and B (adsorbent dosage) are assigned as rank 3 and 2, respectively. The graph shows the effect of factors at each level on the output response (adsorption capacity) in terms of ηm. To determine which factor has the highest contribution at a particular level, the graph shows a high ηm. This value is considered as an optimal level to optimize the adsorption capacity of the composite. According to Fig. 8 (c), the optimal conditions for the adsorption process are pH 9, an adsorbent dosage of 0.01 mg, and an initial dye concentration of 150 mg/L. At these optimum conditions, the adsorption capacity is 233.54 mg/g (S/N = 47.36). The lower adsorbent dosage is favorable for the higher adsorption capacity of the composite due to very few MO molecules per unit of adsorbent [51]. The initial dye concentration used in the experiment acts as a driving force for the mass transfer resistance of the molecule between solid and liquid phases [7]. With a rise in the initial dye concentration, the difference in concentration between the two substances also grows, leading to a stronger interaction between the material being absorbed and the material doing the adsorbing. This ultimately leads to a greater adsorption capacity. Similarly, composite has a high potential for MO dye adsorption at pH >6. The dye solution is affected by changing pH; for example, pH < 6 causes the nitrogen atom in the dye solution to generate a positive charge. This results in electrostatic repulsion between the dye solution and the composite [52]. Therefore, at higher pH, adsorption capacity is increased due to strong electrostatic attraction between the dye molecule and the composite.

As described in Section 2.11, ANOVA is also performed. The model fitness and its significance are determined by the results of ANOVA as shown in Table 4 using F-value (Fisher's test), p-value (probability of occurrence, and R2 (coefficient of determination). The significance of the parameters can be measured using F-ratio, which helps investigate the variance of all the input parameters, including the error term at a desired significance level, α [20,53]. The factors having a p-value >0.05 were considered to be insignificant. As evident from Table 4, interactions between all the factors are statistically insignificant, and all the input parameters significantly affect the adsorption capacity of PANI-FOCD.

Additionally, a mathematical model that depicts the connection between input parameters and adsorption capacity has been formulated through the application of regression analysis. The regression model is given in Eq. (16). The value of the coefficient of determination (R2) comes out to be 86.7 %, which implies that the variation in the input parameters explains 86.7 % variation in the adsorption capacity.
(16)
Adsorption capacity , mg / g = 52.8 + 2.71 × A – 3913 × B + 1.01 × C

After attaining the best conditions to maximize the composite's adsorption capacity, a confirmation test is conducted to confirm the accuracy of the optimization outcome. The experimental and projected values can assure the accuracy of the outcome of the S/N ratio. If these values closely resemble each other, it substantiates the validation of the optimization result. It can be noted that the combination of input parameters of experiment number 25 of Table 2 is also the optimal combination. Therefore, experimental adsorption capacity values and their corresponding S/N ratio are 233.54 mg/L and 47.36, respectively. The Eq. (17) calculates the predicted value of the S/N ratio [22].

This study employs three different isotherm models, as outlined in Section 2.12. To achieve optimal outcomes, the Microsoft Excel Solver add-in tool, in combination with a non-linear optimization approach, has been utilized. By computing the regression coefficient (R2) and chi-square (χ2), the best-fit model is identified using the Eq. (18) and Eq. (19) [7];

are experimental, calculated and mean value of adsorption capacity (mg/g). All the parameters of three isotherm models are described in Table S2. Among all the isotherm models, the Langmuir model best fits with R2 of 0.99 and minimum χ2 of 10.16, as shown in Fig. 9(a). One distinctive aspect of the Langmuir model is its separation factor, which indicates the favourability of adsorption based on its numerical value. Adsorption is favorable if the value of RL is between 0 and 1; if RL is equal to zero and 1, then adsorption is irreversible and linear; if it is >1, adsorption is unfavorable. RL can be determined by Eq. (20).
(20)

1
1 +
K L
C i

The determined RL value for dye adsorption onto PANI-FOCD lies between 0 and 1, indicating a favorable adsorption process. The highest achievable adsorption capacity for the ternary composite is measured at 462.96 mg/g. This capacity is more than the adsorption capacity of PANI-FO, which has been reported in our recent literature [7]. The increase in adsorption capacity can be due to the availability of more active sites because of the incorporation of CDs. The aromatic ring in MO and the conjugated structure in the CDs may generate a π-π interaction in addition to ionic interactions in the MO dye [54].

All the constants of the adsorption kinetics model are given in Table S3. The calculated values of adsorption capacity are found to be very close to experimental values, and its linear graph shows high regression coefficients for all the concentrations of dyes. Since the pseudo-second-order model fits very well (Fig. 9(b)), it shows that chemical adsorption of dye is occurring. Moreover, the intra-particle diffusion model also shows higher regression coefficients for all concentrations. This model comprises three primary stages: (I) external diffusion or boundary layer diffusion, involving the solute's diffusion through the boundary layer situated between the adsorbate and the adsorbent; (II) internal diffusion or pore film diffusion, encompassing the solute's movement into the adsorbent's pores; and (III) the equilibrium stage, where the adsorbate and adsorbent interact either chemically or physically, leading to a deceleration of intra-particle diffusion [55]. Step (II) is considered the slowest rate or rate-limiting step as compared to steps (I) and step (III). Fig. 9 (c) shows multi-linearity plots for all the initial concentrations of dyes. Two linear graphs have been observed for concentrations ranging from 10 mg/L to 100 mg/L, whereas three linear graphs can be seen for 150 and 200 mg/L. This multiple nature of plots suggests that more than one step is affecting the adsorption process. If the plots traverse through the origin, it signifies that intra-particle diffusion singularly dictates the rate-controlling step. In the case of Fig. 9 (c), where two or more linear graphs are observable, it implies that boundary layer diffusion and equilibrium adsorption potentially influence adsorption to a certain extent, particularly for lower initial dye concentrations [56]. However, for higher dye concentrations, the diffusion depends on micropores, mesopores, and macropores of adsorbent. The lower rate constants in the second and third steps represent slower adsorption, which means the achievement of equilibrium. The rate constant values for concentration 10–100 mg/L are lower, which represents slow adsorption as compared to higher concentrations of dye. Additionally, the value of boundary layer thickness increases, which represents the adsorption and loading of dye molecules on the surface of the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption of dyes and adsorption mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the outcomes presented above, it is evident that the ternary composite PANI-FOCD displays promising potential as an efficient adsorbent for the successful removal of MO dye. In this section, the adsorption efficiency of other dyes, including cationic and anionic dyes, has been explored. Five types of dyes have been studied: three cationic dyes and two anionic dyes, namely, BG, RhB, MV, MR, and MO. The information on the physical properties and molecular structure of all dyes has been given in Table S3. The adsorption efficiency of anionic dyes having a concentration of 50 mg/L was found to be 99 % and 79 % using 0.01 g of adsorbent at pH 7, as mentioned in Fig. 10 (a) and (b). Whereas the adsorption efficiency of cationic dyes is determined to be 85 %, 80 %, and 0.5 % for MV, BG, and RhB having 50 mg/L of concentration. This phenomenon arises due to the positive charges present on the composite's surface, which lead to electrostatic repulsion between the dye and PANI-FOCD. These results imply that PANI-FOCD has more potential to remove anionic dyes, having 15–20 times higher adsorption efficiency than cationic dyes. These results can also be justified from the point of zero charge test, as shown in Fig. 10 (c). When the pH of the solution is greater than the pKa of the MO dye (pKa = 3.97), most MO molecules are in their deprotonated, anionic form. This is because the pH is higher than the pKa, which means the solution is basic. In this form, MO molecules carry a negative charge. At the same time, the point of zero charge of the adsorbent is 4.9, indicating that below this pH, the adsorbent surface is positively charged, and above this pH, it's negatively charged. If the pH of the dye solution is greater than point of zero charge, the surface of the composite protonated to positive charges due to the incorporation of polyaniline in FOCD and results in more attraction of anionic dye as compared to cationic dye [57]. However, cationic dyes (BG and RhB) have a pka value close to 2, which means that as the pH increases beyond the dye's pka, a greater proportion of cationic dye molecules convert to their anionic form, which may decrease the attraction between the dye and the negatively charged adsorbent surface. This could reduce adsorption efficiency at a pH level close to 7. However, the presence of carboxyl hydroxyl groups due to CDs, as confirmed by FTIR results, the attraction or adsorption of cationic dye can also occur at pH > 4.9.

The FTIR data supports and clarifies the adsorption of dyes on the composite. FTIR of the composite after MO adsorption shows peaks at 3447 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1, which correspond to the OH and NH peaks, are transferred to a higher wavenumber, as seen in Fig. 10 (d). This suggests that the hydroxyl and amino groups of the composites and the MO dye potentially interact owing to hydrogen bonding. The methyl group of MO dye displays distinct peaks at 1423 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1, while the presence of sulfonic vibration can be observed by two peaks at 1235 cm−1 and 692 cm−1. The peaks at 1480 cm−1 and 1300 cm−1 represent the bending peak of CH2 and the stretching of the single bond between aromatic amine and C, respectively. The stretching of CN in the PANI polaron structure at 1238 cm−1 can be linked to the binding of MO dye to the composite through π-π interactions. Furthermore, following the adsorption process, a noticeable rise in the peak at 1158 cm−1 is observed, corresponding to the stretching of the sulfonate group (SO) and can be attributed to electron delocalization. This rise in peak could potentially lead to an electrostatic attraction between the MO dye and the composite material. The carbonyl groups in composite (CO) act as electron donors, and the aromatic rings of dye behave as electron acceptors. The results of FTIR analysis data show that the carbonyl group peak has been increased (1730 cm−1), which suggests the presence of n- π* transitions and is also consistent with the literature [58]. The schematic diagram of adsorption mechanism is shown in Fig. 10(e).

Moreover, the molecular size of dyes also significantly impacts the adsorption process. As per the literature, the density functional theory is quite helpful in determining the molecular size of a composite and supporting the mechanism for dye adsorption on the composite [54]. The molecular length and size of MO dye are much smaller than other dyes (molecular length 1.8–1.34 nm, and molecular width 1.18 to 0.88 nm [54]). The BET and DFT results showed that the pores radius of PANI-FOCD composite is about 1.603 nm, which is comparable to the molecular size of MO dye. The adsorbent contains nano-sized pores, and its functional groups or active sites are disseminated throughout the outer and inner surfaces. Therefore, MO can be entrapped inside the pores of adsorbent and adsorbed on the surface of adsorbents, which enhances its adsorption efficiency compared to other anionic dyes. The intra-particle diffusion model also confirms this mechanism.

Batch adsorption has been considered effective for the removal of contaminants from wastewater. Yet, because of their limited practical usefulness, column operations are preferred in industries because they consist of constant flow of adsorbate through a bed of adsorbent at a consistent rate, which results in a higher concentration gradient across the interface of adsorbate and adsorbent. A breakthrough profile has investigated the performance of an adsorbate in a column adsorption study. Fig. 11 (a) illustrates the MO dye breakthrough profile of PANI-FOCD of column performed with 30 mg/L of initial dye concentration at pH 8 passed through a column at different flow rates like 0.2 mL/min, 0.5 mL/min, and 1 mL/min. Typically, the breakthrough and exhaustion points are defined as the points at which Cf/Co approaches 0.1 and 1.0, respectively (where Cf is the effluent dye concentration and Co is the inlet dye concentration). Initially, the feed dye solution of 30 mg/L is passed through the inlet of a column; at this stage, the dye is adsorbed most swiftly and effectively by the upper few layers of the virgin composite. As observed in Fig. 11 (a), no or very little dye was detected in the effluent till 50 min for almost all the flow rates due to a larger quantity of composite. As a result, the principal adsorption zone, or MTZ, is reached at the top of the column. As the time increases, the dye concentration in effluents also increases and follows a smooth and constant growth in profile till 3000 min for 0.2 mL/min, 2700 min for 0.5 mL/min, and 2200 for 1 mL/min of flow rate. The MTZ descends to the column, resulting in composite saturation, which means that the effluent concentration of dye becomes almost equal to the influent concentration. The composite saturation occurs faster for a higher flow rate than for a lower flow rate because of the increased mass transfer rate and rapid elution of MO dye. The breakthrough profile is analyzed by various parameters as described by Eq. (21) to Eq. (24) [59].

where L is the length of bed, ts and tb are saturation and breakthrough time. All the parameters for different flowrates are given in Table 6 . After saturation, 40 mL of 0.01 M HCl solution is passed through the column for the regeneration process. The eluent sample is collected, and its concentration is analyzed every 10 mL using UV–Vis spectroscopy. This process is repeated for four adsorption-desorption cycles. The percentage of dye desorbed is found to be 99–90 % % in cycles 1–4, and its adsorption efficiency has been reduced to 92 %, as shown in Fig. 11 (b). The H+ ion in the eluent reduces the interaction between composite and dye, or Cl- ions form a complex with dye molecules and release into the solution. The primary pathway in the regeneration process of composites employing acidic medium desorbing agents, owing to the protonation of composite active sites in desorbing pollutants. Further, Fig. S6 shows early saturation of the composite as the regeneration cycle increases, which might be due to the alteration in the chemical and physical structure of the composite as compared to the first cycle.

Fig. 11 (c) shows the adsorbent cost of treating 1 L of dye under different reuse cycles, calculated by the equations described in our previous research paper [4]. The cost per liter (C/L) of absorbent, purchasing cost of adsorbent, desorption cost, incineration cost, and distillation cost are provided in the supplementary file. The cost of generating 1 g of composite includes aniline monomer (0.013 $), iron oxide nanoparticle (0.014$), KPS ( 6 ×
10
− 4
$), HCl (0.24$), ethanol (0.099$), and apple peel ( 1.6 ×
10
− 6
$).

In a developing country such as India, it's common to find that the EBITDA margin average for several leading textile companies typically falls within the range of 10 % to 27 % [60]. With lower profitability across textile industries, literature has mentioned that these lower profitable industries are only willing to afford the contaminant removal process if the additional investment ranges from $4–$6 per m3 of the wastewater [4]. In the present study, the total cost per liter is 5 $/L (5 $ per m3), showing that PANI-FOCD has great potential for contaminant removal and water purification across industries. One of the challenges observed in this study is that the composite adsorbed only anionic dyes, whereas very little adsorption is observed for cationic dyes. Consequently, our subsequent research will focus on enhancing the composite's efficacy in eliminating a broader array of pollutants. This ongoing study is anticipated to be detailed in forthcoming reports.

This study demonstrates the effective integration of iron oxide nanoparticles onto a polymer matrix through carbon dot modification. The ternary composite resulting from this synthesis displays favorable chemical, physical, and thermal stability, showcasing significant promise for effectively removing dyes from aqueous solutions. The successful inclusion of carbon dots onto iron oxide nanoparticles has been verified via FTIR, XPS, and UV–Vis/PL spectra analysis. Further, uniform dispersion of nanoparticles is observed by SEM/TEM analysis. The synthesized composite shows enhanced surface area after adding CDs, i.e., 112.3 m2/g. The optimization of input parameters to achieve maximum adsorption capacity is also carried out using Taguchi's approach, and it has been found that pH = 9, adsorbent dosage = 0.01, and initial dye concentration = 150 mg/L is the optimal condition resulting in optimum adsorption capacity. Moreover, it has also been discovered that the adsorption capacity is notably influenced by pH, adsorbent dosage, and initial dye concentration. The composite can adsorb small-sized MO dye particles and aligns with the Langmuir isotherm model, demonstrating a remarkable maximum adsorption capacity of 462.96 mg/g. Furthermore, the composite adheres to the pseudo-second-order and intra-particle diffusion models, supporting the inference that the adsorption process is of chemisorption nature, and boundary layer diffusion may serve as the rate-limiting step. The adsorption mechanism has been studied by FTIR results, suggesting that dye forms electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interaction with PANI-FOCD composite. The investigation on column adsorption indicated that the highest capacity for adsorption within the column bed is 198.6 mg/g when using a dye solution of 30 ppm at a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. The synthesized composite offers economic feasibility with substantially reduced treatment costs and holds the potential for being employed in industries for water purification and removing contaminants.

Momina Momina: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Kafeel Ahmad: Investigation, Supervision. Zahid A. Khan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
